author: dannycoll

body:
Using this get time thing is just a way to demonstrate if a part of a component gets re-rendered here. It's not about using this in a real world example. I figured out that in Vue 2 there is no way to solve it. As soon as you use slots whenever data changes the corresponding components get re-rendered. Even if it is keyed correctly. In Vue 3 there is a directive called `v-memo` that might be used to solve it.

I switched to a complete different approach passing components as parameters which then get loaded dynamically via `<component :is=[componentName]>`. Works well and doesn't re-render. So for anyone else facing the same issue trying to improve looped data component rendering this is the way to go.

Cheers!

body:
As much as I appreciate the answer, it does not clearly indicate whether there is or there isn't a way to fulfill the aforementioned use-case. The following scenario does not apply any transition: ```
``` However, the following code does apply the transition **but only when the modal shows up and not when it gets closed**. ```
``` The main point I am trying to raise here is to look for a solution when there is a parent `
` wrapping any transitions. Why cannot we have a feature that, once parent `v-if` is changed to a falsy value, any transitions inside will trigger their `leave` attributes before completely disappearing from the DOM? Doesn't it sound logical to have `disappear` in opposite to `appear`? If there is the way to achieve the above in a different, already existing way - please let me know.
author: DamianGlowala

body:
Your desired `disappear` behavior isn't implemented right now, and likely won't as it is much more complex than `appear`.

You see, where with `appear`, the transition component can do the in-transition independent of the the parent chain, `disappear` would mean that somehow element removals up the parent chain would have to be halted until all nested transitions have disappeared.

While not impossible, this is much harder to implement and requires much more additional code. For that reason, we so far have left the management of such scenarios to user land.

author: demianchuk

body:
A few more thoughts:

- Making it harder to access the component tree does not make it impossible. Therefore it provides a false sense of security, which, IMO, is more dangerous than showing the dev tools to all developers.
- So, actually, I initially advocated for not providing the ability to turn off dev tools in production at all.
- Because front-end code is not secure at all. JS files are minified for size concerns, not for security. Visitors can always inspect the website's front-end logic. Sensitive info and business logic should be secured at the back end.
- Nevertheless, in Vue 3, it is possible to completely turn off Vue Devtools in production by setting the compile-time flag `__VUE_PROD_DEVTOOLS__` to `false`. But again, this is to reduce the bundle size, not to secure the app.

body:
The template ref-removal behavior in 2.7.0-alpha differs from 2.6, but it's consistent with Vue 3 ([demo](https://codepen.io/tony19/pen/xxYmqOo?editors=1010)) and with `@vue/composition-api` in Vue 2.6 ([demo](https://codepen.io/tony19/pen/dydwvOY)).

The behavior was introduced in 460856510d1a6356194e10f887df9b057297c07e:

https://github.com/vuejs/vue/blob/460856510d1a6356194e10f887df9b057297c07e/src/core/vdom/modules/template-ref.ts#L35

Changing that line's `null` to `undefined` would restore the original behavior, but would cause inconsistencies with Vue 3. I think it was actually intentional, as the [unit tests even explicitly check for `null`](https://github.com/vuejs/vue/blob/460856510d1a6356194e10f887df9b057297c07e/test/unit/features/template-ref.spec.ts#L61). Perhaps migration docs should be added.

body: This is intentional. `ComponentInternalInstance`, as the name suggests, is internal. The fact that the Composition API plugin exposed a mocked internal instance was a mistake. It leads to patterns that break composability and has subtle differences from the Vue 3 behavior that can't be fully compatible. In 2.7, the returned instance only exposes `proxy`, which is the equivalent of `this` in Options API. Code that relies on any other properties should be refactored.

body:
I think it's caused by https://github.com/vuejs/vue/blob/main/packages/compiler-sfc/src/compileScript.ts#L1838

Event handler like `$emit('update:a');` is recognized as a TypeScript type annotation syntax and passed to `babelParser.parseExpression`, which caused an error because it's a *statement* not an *expression*.

A possible fix is #12594 , which strips the strings in the code before detecting type syntax.

And I've found that Vue3 has the same problem. I think the fix can be ported to vuejs/core too.

body:
In vue3:

```
export const getCurrentInstance: () => ComponentInternalInstance | null = () =>
  currentInstance || currentRenderingInstance
```

in @vue/composition-api

```
let currentInstance: ComponentInternalInstance | null = null
// ....
export function getCurrentInstance() {
  return currentInstance
}
```

They both returned `ComponentInternalInstance`. So vue2.7 are missing the following important function & props：

+ state
  - data
  - props
  - slots
+ function
  - emit
  - update

author: uyarn

body:
1. `@vue/composition-api`'s implementation is not the source of truth - 2.7 has intentional deviations from it.

2. Only exposing `proxy` is intentional, because other properties on the internal instance is considered, well, **internal**. You should refactor any code that relies on anything other than `proxy`.

   This is necessary because relying on the internal instance properties when using Composition API is a sign that you are misusing it. It makes your code less reusable AND introduces risk of compatibility with future versions of Vue. We exposed `getCurrentInstance` mostly for official libraries that need additional internal access, not for userland application code. It was mistakenly documented in WIP v3 docs but is no longer considered a public API.

3. Vue 3 will likely start showing warnings when you rely on properties other than `proxy` soon.

export default {
setup() {
    const windowSize = useWindowSize();
    return { windowSize }
}
```

body:
There's not really a way for Vue itself to reliably detect that multiple versions of it is being used - since this can depend on your dependency tree, package manager used, build tool used, and dep install order over time.

Even if we special this case, there could be other errors when you have multiple copies of Vue being bundled.

One way to avoid this is to always remove your lockfiles and do a fresh install when upgrading major deps like Vue.

body:
Bumped into similar case: was getting `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'depend')` after updating to Vue 2.7.16.

Was caused by "catch-all keys" [Proxy](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Proxy) instance being passed as component property. Proxy was returning unexpected value for `__ob__` field introduced in [2.7.16 here ](https://github.com/vuejs/vue/commit/0ad8e8d94f3a3bf4429f25850c85a6bbb2b81364#diff-2e26135213b820cfd0eaba327405d14d405a4eee7d14a5c4acfcc2c84ef14692R154).

Solution was to update proxy implementation to ignore `__ob__` key.

**Edit: Solutions found! https://github.com/vuejs/language-tools/discussions/5629#discussioncomment-14447960**

This was so difficult to dig up, THANK YOU for the workaround (using `shims-vue.d.ts` was also ineffective for us)! Unsure why we are hitting this despite a fix being merged in 2022 (using `"vue": "^3.5.21"` and `"vue-tsc": "^3.0.7"`), but I am adding some error output for posterity and to guide other desperate people scouring the web. 

The type mismatches looking slightly different for us (making it harder to find this old low-engagement ticket): 

```
 error TS2769: No overload matches this call.
  Overload 2 of 3, '(setup: (props: Record<string, any>, ctx: { attrs: Data; slots: Readonly<InternalSlot
s>; emit: (event: string, ...args: any[]) => void; expose: <Exposed extends Record<string, any> = Record<
...>>(exposed?: Exposed | undefined) => void; }) => RenderFunction | Promise<...>, options?: (Pick<...> &
 { ...; }) | undefined): DefineSetupFnComponent<...>', gave the following error.
    Object literal may only specify known properties, and 'name' does not exist in type '(props: Record<s
tring, any>, ctx: { attrs: Data; slots: Readonly<InternalSlots>; emit: (event: string, ...args: any[]) =>
 void; expose: <Exposed extends Record<string, any> = Record<...>>(exposed?: Exposed | undefined) => void
; }) => RenderFunction | Promise<...>'.
```

Removing the `name` prop just changed the error to say `Object literal may only specify known properties, and 'components' does not exist in type '(props: Record<string, any>, ctx: { attrs: Data; slots: Readonly<InternalSlots>; emit: (event: string, ...args: any[]) => void; expose: <Exposed extends Record<string, any> = Record<...>>(exposed?: Exposed | undefined) => void; }) => RenderFunction | Promise<...>'.ts-plugin(2769)`

Removing `components` changed it to `Object literal may only specify known properties, and 'props' does not exist in type '(props: Record<string, any>, ctx: { attrs: Data; slots: Readonly<InternalSlots>; emit: (event: string, ...args: any[]) => void; expose: <Exposed extends Record<string, any> = Record<...>>(exposed?: Exposed | undefined) => void; }) => RenderFunction | Promise<...>'.ts-plugin(2769)`

…and so on and so forth.

_Edit: It doesn't actually fix the type inference of computed properties. For that, manually overriding the type was required: https://stackoverflow.com/a/70983629/2836299_

~The other manifestation of this issue is that computed variables are incorrectly inferred as `() -> ActualType` instead of `ActualType` resulting in errors like `Argument of type '() => ActualType' is not assignable to parameter of type 'ActualType'` and `error TS2322: Type 'Computed' is not assignable to type ActualType`, `error TS2339: Property 'computedVariableProp' does not exist on type 'Computed'.`, and ` error TS2488: Type 'Computed' must have a '[Symbol.iterator]()' method that returns an iterator.`, which I documented here: https://github.com/vuejs/language-tools/discussions/5629~

body:
Fundamental problem is that `ej2-vue` is applying different behavior based on the presence of the global `h` API: https://github.com/syncfusion/ej2-vue-ui-components/blob/master/components/base/src/component-base.ts#L28

This is wrong because the presence of global `h` is never guaranteed to be the difference between Vue 2 and Vue 3. It should use Vue.version to check behavior difference instead.

body:
I am not the authority to say what is to be expected.  I feel that the behavior should be the same though, or the documentation should clearly state what the differences are.

Having said that, my _opinion_ is: 
* Setting the root properties on a reactive object should fire both deep and shallow watchers, as Vue3 does, but Vue 2 current does not fire either one.
* Setting the root properties on a reactive ref object should fire only the deep watchers.  Vue 3 does this as well, but Vue 2 fires both the shallow and deep watcher.

So IMHO Vue3 is correct and Vue2 is wrong.

I will also add that  I was using Vue Composition API plugin before migrating to Vue 2.7, and the reactivity behavior changed.
author: Flamenco

body:
If things are left as they are, and not considered to be bugs, I would suggest documenting in Vue 2.7 to state:

# Reactive Objects
Do not using an empty reactive object, or modify a root property on a reactive object, if you expect to watch for any changes (deep or not).

# Ref Objects
Using a ref object will fire both non-deep and deep watchers when modifying root properties, but this will not occur in Vue 3.  In Vue3 only deep watchers will fire unless the entire object is swapped out.

body:
Were you using `@vue/composition-api` before with 2.6?

This behavior is aligned with Vue 3 - when you directly watch a ref, the watcher only fires when its `.value` changes. In this case it's always the same array (for performance reasons), so the watcher never fires. I'm not sure what caused `@vue/composition-api` to fire the watcher in this case, but the Vue 3 behavior should be considered the source of truth here.

Typically, the recommended way to react to array refs change is using `onUpdated()`. If you only want to do something when the list has really changed, you can use a deep watcher instead (there's no performance concern here because HTMLElements and component instances won't be traversed by deep watch).

Vue 2 JSX transform doesn't support transforming `onXXX` listeners, nor does Vue 2 accept `onXXX` props for emit-declared events. Basically, this is a feature that does not exist in Vue 2 and we do not intend to backport this since it's a niche feature and doesn't justify the effort needed to implement and maintain it.

@JorisAerts your issue is valid but is unrelated to this one.

body:
This issue has been around for a long time and has been asked in Vue CLI and Vue Loader :
https://github.com/vuejs/vue-cli/issues/7130
https://github.com/vuejs/vue-loader/issues/1975 —— In this issue , typescript source maps is also abnormal.
Since the release of Chrome 99(Vue 2.7 has not yet been released), all Vue 2 Source maps have been abnormal, which I think is related to this.
http://developer.chrome.com/en/blog/new-in-devtools-99/
In particular, Vite does not have this issue.In general, `*.js` is also normal.

这个问题已经很久了，并且在 Vue CLI、 Vue Loader 中都有反馈：
https://github.com/vuejs/vue-cli/issues/7130
https://github.com/vuejs/vue-loader/issues/1975 —— 这里提到了 typescript 的源码映射似乎也一直有问题
自从 Chrome 99 发布之后（此时 Vue 2.7 尚未发布），全部的 Vue 2 的 Source Map 便出现了异常， 我想应该与此有关。
http://developer.chrome.com/zh/blog/new-in-devtools-99/
需要特别说明的是，Vite 并不存在这个问题。一般来说， *.js 也是正常的。

---

To display the Source Map, I configured Webpack as： `devtool: 'source-map'`

body:
This is intentional. The instance you get from `getCurrentInstance()` is **internal** and your code should never rely on it. Also don't rely on `_provided`, as the underscore suggests it's also internal. Use the public APIs!

Using internal APIs means your code can break in future patch releases.

body:
This is expected behavior. Computed refs must be created synchronously within `setup()` to be "linked" to its owner component so that they can be stopped on component unmount.

Creating computed like this will surely lead to memory leaks. In short: don't do it.

感觉已经说的挺明确了啊……
watch is always in working，but deactivited component can't get propsData passed from father，so that object prop in child component never be changed in deactivited, and when you call `this.condition = {}` nothing happen. 
But in activited hook the new object can be passed by father, so `this.condition = {}` can be detected.
watch is always in working，object doesn't be changed, then property in this object with `deep:true` option can be detected.

body:
you problem is that you copy the props of `InvoiceItem` once on mount into the internal `invoice` object, ```js data() { return { invoice: { number: this.initialNumber, client: this.initialClient, amount: this.initialAmount, status: this.initialStatus, }, ``` ...but then later never update it when the props change. And you use this object, not the props, to print the "current" values in the template: ```html

{{invoice.client}}

{{invoice.amount}}

``` If you, for example, just print the props values directly in the template, you can see the updates: ```html

{{initialClient}}

{{initialAmount}}

```

body:
I think it's expected behavior.

`proxyRefs()` does not return a reactive object.
Its type signature is `ShallowUnwrapRef<T>`, while `reactive()` returns an `UnwrapNestedRefs<T>`

It only happens to be the case that the `toRefs()`'s internal implementation can handle `ShallowUnwrapRef<T>`'s structure. But `toRefs()`'s documented usage is only to handle reactive objects.
So I think it's better to create a custom utility function to handle it than to reuse `toRefs()` for this purpose.

body:
I've got a workaround for this issue that should unblock folks.

I took a look at the `diff` between 2.7.5 and 2.7.6, where this broke: https://github.com/vuejs/vue/compare/v2.7.5...v2.7.6

I think the key is the change to the `Vue` types here: https://github.com/vuejs/vue/compare/v2.7.5...v2.7.6#diff-502307d78edff0c6640563ac0f7dcc5c0ec02ee71b8da96c8d54d5c0ab3373ff

![Screen Shot 2022-10-17 at 14 57 36](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/630449/196281995-4776256e-471e-4440-9391-a7d9687fd4e6.png)

By applying this patch (e.g., via [`patch-package`](https://github.com/ds300/patch-package) or `yarn`'s native patching), I was able to get past the error:

```patch
diff --git a/node_modules/@types/vuelidate/vue.d.ts b/node_modules/@types/vuelidate/vue.d.ts
index 64e61c2..0fdeebc 100755
--- a/node_modules/@types/vuelidate/vue.d.ts
+++ b/node_modules/@types/vuelidate/vue.d.ts
@@ -6,7 +6,7 @@ import { Validation } from './vuelidate'
 
 declare module 'vue/types/vue' {
     type ValidationProperties<V> = {
-        [P in Exclude<keyof V, '$v'>]?: Validation & ValidationProperties<V[P]> & ValidationEvaluation
+        [P in Exclude<keyof V, '$v' | '$parent' | '$root' | '$children'>]?: Validation & ValidationProperties<V[P]> & ValidationEvaluation
     }
 
     interface ValidationGroups {
```

I used @AlbinoDrought's example repo to prove that this now builds: https://github.com/blimmer/vue-12750-repro/actions/runs/3268574902/jobs/5375141277

https://github.com/AlbinoDrought/vue-12750-repro/pull/1

I haven't spent too much time figuring out exactly why this works, but it kind of makes sense since `$parent`, `$root`, and `$children` all reference `Vue` in their types.

I don't currently have the bandwidth to open a PR to fix this in `DefinitelyTyped`, but if someone has time, that would be awesome! I hope this helps others get unstuck in the meantime ❤️

body:
This is a known limitation of Vue 2 and cannot be fixed. The root cause is the effect has no way to track anything given the expression `store?.a`:

- `store` is a local variable binding so its access cannot be tracked
- `?.a` accesses a property that doesn't exist yet. In Vue 3 this can be tracked via Proxy traps, but in Vue 2 it cannot be tracked.

The workaround is similar to using `data()` in Options API: always declare root properties that you expect to use before observing them.

Either way, definitely not a Vue core issue.

Also, `vue-ssr-carousel` can avoid this by shipping a separate ESM build by using `libraryTarget: 'module'` in its webpack config, then specify it in the `module` field in `package.json`.

body:
This is because the return type of `data()` now needs to account for unwrapping of potential nested refs. The unwrapping results in a mapped type of the class instance type, which cannot preserve private properties.

Unfortunately this seems to be a case of correctness vs. inconvenience (you can workaround this by casting `this.foo as Foo`) - the unwrapping is technically correct and necessary, but TypeScript currently doesn't provide a way to create a mapped class instance type while preserving the class' private properties.

body:
Unfortunately making the order consistent with Vue 3 can cause unintended breakage. (See #12821 / #12822)

Root cause is that `setup()` requires props to be resolved first, however in Vue 2, props default initializer functions have access to `this`, and some users rely on properties injected from global `beforeCreate` hooks in these functions.

Vue 3 doesn't have this problem because Vue 3 props default initializer functions do not have access to `this`.

body:
This is expected behavior and consistent with Vue 3.

Note your example of using a ref is slightly different from `() => store.state.someArray`.

- In the case of `ref([])`, the watcher only fires if the ref's value changes, i.e. the array is replaced. It does not watch deep mutations of the array itself.
- In the case of `() => store.state.someArray`, the watcher only fires if `someArray` is replaced. Similarly it does *not* watch deep mutations.

If you want to watch array mutations, you need to explicitly use a deep watcher.

The Composition API plugin behavior is different from Vue 3 and thus should be considered a bug.

It's in `vue-loader`.
Vue 2.7+ also applies js loaders to compiled `<template>` code, and among them is `thread-loader`.
Vue 2.6 template compilation doesn't go through js loaders, thus no problem.

body: This is currently a non-goal because Vue 2's template-to-render-fn compilation was written in a way that does not perform any JS expression parsing (it is done in a separate pass with other tools). body:
This is an edge case due to Vue 2's new property addition check working differently compared to Vue 3.

In Vue 3 we use Proxy for proper detection, but in Vue 2 we do this by registering the parent object itself as the change source. The `deep` flag is also causing the watch callback to always run as long as any dependency change. So unfortunately this is an edge case due to Vue 2's reactivity limitations.

body:
Your problem is that in `main.js` you're using `import Vue from 'vue/dist/vue'` to instantiate your application.

Then inside your components, you're importing `provide` and `inject` straight from `vue`.

You need to use same import location, otherwise they can't connect with each other or your main application (hence the errors).

Usually, it's recommended to use an alias in your Vite/Webpack file to resolve `vue: "vue/dist/some.vue.build.js"` to ensure consistency.

The reason the `vue.runtime.esm.js` worked was because that's the script that importing straight from `vue` resolves to.

body:
You have to `dedupe` vue in your client to avoid two copies of vue being included.

This happens because the component is loading vue from the component folder while the client is loading its own vue version. You have to ensure they both load the same vue copy. In practice, this doesn't happen when you install the input-test library as an npm package, here it happens because it's a local link.

You can find more information in issues like

- https://github.com/vuejs/core/issues/2600
- https://github.com/vuejs/core/issues/6984
author: posva

body:
@posva Actually this DOES happen when we install the package as an npm package. Our situation is that we are building a component library which is published to GitHub Packages npm registry and when we install it to another project this issue occurs.

I made the reproduction with a local link just so that the problem would be visible, as the packages we publish are private to the company only.

Note that on the `vite.config.js` of the component we are deduping Vue and also externalizing it, therefore it is not being included in the final build, so I don't think that's the problem.

I also said that when we build the component with Options API it works normally, so if two copies of Vue were to be the problem, then it would not matter if we are using Composition or Options API, the problem would happen anyway.

body:
There are no plans to add `setCurrentInstance()` to Vue 3 so this would still break in Vue 3. I don't know where `store` comes from but in composition API, you would ideally have a `useStore()` function called before `onMounted()`. It would internally use `inject()` to get the actual store.

In short, the thing you want to write **shouldn't be necessary in the first place**, so it's worth having a reproduction (without Nuxt) of what isn't working right now. Otherwise, this should be brought to Nuxt attention instead.
author: posva

body:
@pi0 @danielroe I can make a bug/issue in Nuxt Composition API but will you actually fix it as it's legacy? We can't use Nuxt Bridge now due to many errors.

@posva 
> There are no plans to add `setCurrentInstance()` to Vue 3 so this would still break in Vue 3

How can export break something? Is there a way to restore instance by user action in Vue 3/Vue 2.7? That's why I actually needed to export this in a first place. Maybe useAsyncContext or something like that? I didn't find export anywhere as well.

> you would ideally have a useStore() function called before onMounted()

It's not about the store, it's about calling some instance-related functions inside the store, ex. router, e.t.c.
It could be any other composable/function that is called after the await which requires instance.

> Otherwise, this should be brought to Nuxt attention instead

I don't think they can actually exclude instance usage from their methods because Nuxt CAPI just works like that. The only way here is to restore context somehow or to pass composable results down to stores e.t.c.
author: daniluk4000

body:
Hi @daniluk4000 thanks for making the issue and explaining the problem.

I think also a direct `setCurrentInstance` could be dangerous at least. With Nuxt, we take extra measures to expose `callWithNuxt` that makes it available to the calls in same tick and immediately unsets (it _might_ be an candidate for Vue to introduce). wyt @posva? 

A common issue we have with both Vue 2/3 and CAPI, is that when for any reason only a composable relies on a different Vue build the instances get out of sync (mostly hoisting issues that causes multiple instances of Vue in CJS/ESM cache but can also be pnpm).

I will try to come up with an (vue3) issue and proposal with a new internal API, we can support opt-in sync of vue instances solving many of similar issues for Nuxt.

author: daniluk4000

body:
My workaround for now:
1. Avoid usage of any CAPI in Pinia and use this.$nuxt instead
2. Add "ctx" param to every composable we write with Nuxt CAPI usage
3. If previous two didn't help for some reason, we use setCurrentInstance (from my forked repo)

Don't see any other options for our case.

body:
Tested with both CLI ~4.5.18 and CLI 5.0.8, both correctly transpiles expressions in template expressions.

Please note that for Vue 2.7, **template expressions go through the same loader pipelines as normal JS**. This means syntax lowering will be processed by babel-loader and its config, not through `vue-template-es2015-compiler` anymore. Make sure your babel config or `browserslist` target in `package.json` includes the browser range you want to support.

body:
> But let's keep this issue open so that we can fix it properly in vue core later.

Hi @sodatea!

The problem is caused by not specifying the required prettier version in the `compiler/sfc` package. 

While Prettier 3 is not supported, a simple solution could be to specify the Prettier version in optional dependencies. It would keep prettifying working even if Prettier 3 is installed. 
 
There is a PR that does this: https://github.com/vuejs/vue/pull/13053

The same solution was applied by you before, but it was lost during migration to Vue 2.7: https://github.com/vuejs/component-compiler-utils/commit/aea1b79765f0f6d688e64b7698d5ff62862002d1

body: I think it might be because you directly passed `this.$scopedSlots` into `Inner.data.scopedSlots`, which led to the implicit property `$stable` that was originally defined in `Wrapper.$scopedSlots` also being transferred to `Inner.data.scopedSlots`. So this is why using the spread operator works fine, because the spread operator does not expand implicit properties. ## excepted . ## actually（in your example） ![image](https://github.com/vuejs/vue/assets/76022317/808c91e4-6f22-4b05-9e8d-92cb5a94da12) And then, due to Vue's slot optimization mechanism, it ultimately led to the following logic... ![image](https://github.com/vuejs/vue/assets/76022317/b1afb36b-fd04-4b70-8a35-935bd42d8ce2) So, Vue reused the previous scoped slot object, thereby reducing the rendering overhead.

body: How did you find this problem? I don’t see why one would write a sequence when defining the emit function?
author: posva

body:
> How did you find this problem? I don’t see why one would write a sequence when defining the emit function?

Because of istanbuljs's instrumentation process. The VariableDeclaration code after instrumenting will be compiled to  SequenceExpression.

Like this code:

```vue
<script setup>
const emits = defineEmits(['input']);
</script>
```

after intrument, it will become like this:

```vue
<script setup>
// some unrelated report init code
const emits = (report(0), defineEmits(['input']));
</script>
```

author: DeAntiWang

body:
I'm not convinced that this complexity should be dealt with in Vue - instrumenting raw SFC code just sounds wrong. Only handling sequences also seems like an incomplete fix, as there are other cases where the macros won't be compiled. For example, what if the instrumentation wraps the `defineXXX` macros in conditional branches?

Even just for sequences, the PR also has even more edge cases:

- It assumes the `defineXXX` always is the last in the sequence, so it would fail for
   ```js
   const props = (Date.now(), defineProps(['input']), Date.now())
   ```

- In the multi-declaration case, it also doesn't account for duplicated placeholders:

   ```js
   const foo = 1, props = (Date.now(), defineProps(['input']))
   const bar = 1, emits = (Date.now(), defineEmits(['input']))
   ```

   Generated code will error because two variables named `_` are declared.

In principle, `compileScript` expects to work with the exact code written by the developer, so we do not account for extremely unlikely usage like sequence expressions. But if the goal is to make the logic robust enough that it can handle unknown instrumentation by other tools, then the complexity will explode and I don't think Vue should be responsible for that.

body:
In Vue 2.7, **all [Vue 2 change detection caveats still apply](https://v2.vuejs.org/v2/guide/reactivity#Change-Detection-Caveats).

This means `reactive({})` creates an empty object that won't track newly added properties. To ensure properties can be tracked, you need to initialize them with `null` or `undefined` values just like in `data()`.

author: Elphazy

body:
That is a common 'workaround', but it will trigger a change with an invalid value. For instance, in the above example, it will trigger both 'FOOb' and 'FOOB'. I don't think if you do proper state management that is acceptable.

But more importantly, using the pattern of value + onChange usually work. So if you use this pattern throughout your app, you might feel confident your app is without bugs, and then in this really strange edge case, your app is in an inconsistent state.

author: mitar

body:
Thanks for bringing this up — I've run into the same limitation when trying to combine structural equality with abortable side-effects using `watch`.

The core issue is that the `onCleanup()` is always invoked regardless of whether the user-defined equality says “nothing changed”, which defeats the point of skipping the fetch logic when params/query are semantically the same.

A few workarounds I've tried:

- Keeping the `AbortController` outside the `watch` and manually aborting only when semantic differences are detected. Works, but feels messy and couples too much logic outside the reactive context.
- Using `JSON.stringify(...)` as a reactive dependency (e.g. `computed(() => JSON.stringify(props.query))`) to get strict equality — readable but potentially fragile, especially with ordering and null vs. undefined.

Totally agree that being able to pass a custom `equals` function directly into `watch()` would improve clarity and intent. It would also align more closely with how `reactive()` and some state management libs handle equality.

If the concern is backward compatibility, perhaps this could be added as an opt-in feature — something like:

```ts
watch(source, callback, {
  equals: (a, b) => isEqual(a, b),
})
```

This would preserve existing behavior but give advanced users more control.

Thanks again for documenting the issue so clearly — following this one closely!
